Highlights
----------

*   •
    
    The industrial viability of CO₂RR technology primarily depends on various factors related to [electrocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysts "Learn more about electrocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") performance.
    
*   •
    
    Key criteria include FE, current density, overpotential, energy efficiency, stability, and material cost.
    
*   •
    
    Recent advancements in catalytic materials for CO₂RR with potential industrial applications have been evaluated.
    
*   •
    
    The review also covers CO₂RR's fundamental principles and the economic values of potential CO₂RR products.
    

Abstract
--------

Converting waste or hazardous chemicals into valuable products is a paramount consideration from economic, environmental, and sustainability standpoints. Diverse strategies are under exploration to convert CO₂ into valuable or fine chemicals, encompassing [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), thermo- and photo-catalysis, and chemical fixation. Amid these avenues, the electrochemical CO₂ reduction reaction (CO₂RR) emerges as exceptionally promising, driven by its manifold advantages and the growing accessibility of renewable electricity sources. While CO₂RR has witnessed substantial advancements, most endeavors remain in the proof-of-concept phase, necessitating improved catalytic efficiency and stability to enable industrialization. Realizing the industrial viability of CO₂RR technology mandates meticulous consideration of a myriad of electrocatalyst-related factors. This review delves into critical industrial criteria and recent catalytic materials with the potential to drive CO₂ reduction at an industrial scale. These factors, akin to other catalytic processes, closely relate to catalytic activity, product selectivity, catalyst/system stability, and catalyst cost. In this context, we investigated the criteria that define electrocatalysts as industrially feasible, considering factors such as Faradaic efficiency, [current density](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/current-density "Learn more about current density from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), energy efficiency, stability, overpotential, and the choice of catalyst materials. Furthermore, we highlight prime examples demonstrating high potential for this process and categorize them based on the reaction products. To offer a comprehensive perspective, this review also discusses the fundamental principles of CO₂RR, covering the physicochemical properties of CO₂, cell configurations, electrolyte [compositions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/phase-composition "Learn more about compositions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the role of electrocatalysts. We also address the economic significance of various CO₂RR products.

1\. Introduction
----------------

Increasing the industrial activities is always associated with [fossil fuel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") utilization which is the main cause of increasing the amount of CO₂ emissions. It was breaking a high record of 37.4gigatonnes (Gt) CO₂ in 2023 marking a growth of 1.1% or an increase of 410 million tonnes (Mt) compared to 2022 [\[1\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib1). This massive amount of CO₂ emissions contributes to global warming and climate change [\[2\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib2). The approach of Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) is broadly identified as a critical strategy to solve this environmental issue [\[3\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib3). Currently, a variety of CO₂ utilization facilities are in operation worldwide, with ongoing R&D projects at the various stages of technological development [\[4\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib4). Utilizing the excessive [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")2 waste by converting it into fuels and chemicals is a promising and sustainable approach to reach the net zero target emission. Various conversion methods have been proposed including: photochemical [\[5\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib5), [\[6\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib6), biochemical [\[7\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib7), [\[8\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib8), thermochemical [\[9\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib9), [\[10\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib10), and electrochemical [\[11\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib11), [\[12\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib12) processes. Each strategy offers its unique economic advantages, but also faces its own set of technological challenges. However, the electrocatalytic [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")2 reduction reaction (CO₂RR) stands out as an excellent choice due to its mild reaction conditions and the potential for using low-cost renewable electricity. Significant progress has been made in many aspects related to the [electrochemical reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrochemical-reaction "Learn more about electrochemical reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of CO2 such as [catalyst design](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/catalyst-design "Learn more about catalyst design from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), product selectivities, cell configuration, electrolyte type, and reaction conditions, _etc_. [\[13\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib13), [\[14\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib14), [\[15\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib15), [\[16\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib16), [\[17\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib17), [\[18\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib18), [\[19\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib19), [\[20\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib20), [\[21\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib21). While considerable progress has been made in laboratory-scale experiments, scaling up CO2 electroreduction for industrial applications remains a challenge. Currently, translating the promising lab results into scalable technologies, that can compete in commodity markets, has a high priority. Until now, there are no commercially available CO₂ electroreduction reaction technologies [\[22\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib22), and even the number of reported systems suitable for large-scale commercial application remains limited. While numerous comprehensive reviews have focused on various aspects of CO2RR, ranging from the fundamentals and mechanistic details to gas diffusion electrodes and membranes, cell configuration, techno-economic analyses, and catalyst design, particularly emphasizing high-value-added products like multicarbon compounds and alcohols [\[23\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib23), [\[24\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib24), [\[25\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib25), [\[26\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib26), [\[27\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib27), [\[28\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib28), [\[29\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib29), [\[30\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib30), [\[31\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib31), [\[32\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib32), there remains a significant gap in defining criteria for industrial applications and analyzing examples that demonstrate potential for achieving this objective. Only a few reviews have addressed CO2RR at an industrial scale, and their scope significantly differs from the current review. For instance, X. She and co-workers_,_ examined the industrial chain and scaling up developments focusing on gas diffusion electrodes (GDE) [\[33\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib33). Jin, S., _et al._ and Ruiz-López_, et al.,_ highlighted the progress and challenges in scaling up the selective conversion of CO₂ into CO and C₂+ products, respectively [\[34\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib34), [\[35\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib35). Thus, this review aims to explore the industrial significance of CO₂RR and strategies for bridging the gap between laboratory developments and industrial applications. It will on the key research findings from the past three years across all recent studies on potential industrial catalysts, covering all possible resulting products, their economic value, and the criteria for establishing [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysts "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as viable options for industrial-scale CO2 reduction.

2\. Fundamental principles of electrochemical CO₂RR
---------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. Thermodynamic and kinetic considerations in of CO₂RR

The CO₂ molecule is centrosymmetric and linear, featuring two equivalent C–O [double bonds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/double-bond "Learn more about double bonds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), each with a length of 116.3 pm. The carbon center of the CO₂ molecule is electrophilic due to the presence of the surrounding highly electronegative oxygen atoms. Despite this, there is no overall [dipole moment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/dipole-moment "Learn more about dipole moment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") within the C═O bonds. This absence of dipole moment arises from the molecule's linear configuration, which results in the cancellation of the two bond dipoles. CO₂ is known for its exceptional stability, characterized by a high bond energy of 805 kJ/mol. This stability poses a significant thermodynamic challenge when attempting to convert CO₂ into other products, as it demands a substantial amount of energy to break the robust C-O bonds. The direct decomposition of CO₂ to [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and O₂ results in a substantial enthalpy change of approximately 283.0 kJ/mol [\[36\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib36), [\[37\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib37), [\[38\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib38).CO2g→COg+12O2g;∆H°\=283.0kJ/mol

[Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#tbl0005) displays the standard electrode potentials (E°) of selected CO₂RR half reactions [\[39\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib39). However, it is important to note that the sluggish nature of the CO₂RR necessitates a substantially more negative potential to induce these reactions at a practical rate than what is implied by the _E__o_ values. The electroreduction of CO₂ involves multiple elementary steps, and the initial step often requires overcoming a high [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/reaction-activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") barrier. The first step is the formation of the CO₂ [radical anion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/radical-anion "Learn more about radical anion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO₂•−), which is produced by the addition of an electron to CO₂. This step is kinetically challenging and typically requires a very high negative potential (_e.g._, around −1.9 V _vs._ the standard hydrogen electrode, SHE) to proceed efficiently when considering a non-catalytic surface) [\[40\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib40), [\[41\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib41). To prevent the generation of the highly [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") CO₂•−, catalysts play a crucial role in facilitating the direct conversion of CO₂ into a range of reduction products at lower potentials. Consequently, the development of [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalyst "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the CO₂RR is of paramount importance in reducing kinetic energy barriers and enhancing overall energy efficiency of CO₂ reduction process.

Table 1. E° values for the some selected CO₂RR (V vs. SHE) at 1.0 atm and 25 °C [\[39\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib39).

| CO₂RR half-reactions | E° (V vs. SHE) |
| --- | --- |
| CO2g+2H+aq+2e−→COg+H2Ol | ­0.106 |
| CO2g+2H+aq+2e−→HCOOHl | ­0.199 |
| CO2g+6H+aq+6e−→CH3OHl+H2Ol | 0.030 |
| CO2g+8H+aq+8e−→CH4g+2H2Ol | 0.169 |
| CO2g+12H+aq+12e−→C2H4g+4H2Ol | 0.064 |
| 2CO2g+12H+aq+12e−→CH3CH2OHl+3H2Ol | 0.09 |

The electroreduction of CO₂ involves a [complex reaction mechanism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/complex-reaction-mechanisms "Learn more about complex reaction mechanism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that includes the generation of various reaction intermediates. It involves two (2), four (4), six (6), and eight (8) electron reduction paths, resulting in a diverse array of reduction products. In heterogeneous systems, the crucial determinant of product selectivity lies in the binding [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of primary reaction intermediates such as \*H, \*COOH, \*OCOH, and [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (\* denotes an adsorbed species) onto the catalyst surface [\[42\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib42). The affinity between the catalyst and a specific intermediate can significantly influence the preference for a particular reaction pathway, shaping the final product profile. Therefore, a comprehensive understanding and deliberate control of electrocatalyst surface properties are imperative for enhancing the performance of CO₂RR. Nonetheless, the occurrence of nonselective CO₂ reduction predominantly arises from the competition between H+ and CO₂ at pivotal intermediates, ultimately giving rise to a wide range of reduction products through distinct pathways (refer to [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#tbl0005)). Electroreduction products, such as CO, formate, and various hydrocarbons (_e.g._, methane, ethylene), alcohols, _etc._ are formed through a series [of electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about of electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and protonation steps. Each of these steps has its own rate constants and activation energies. The presence of multiple competing pathways with different kinetics complicates the selectivity of the process. In general, the [electrochemical reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrochemical-reaction "Learn more about electrochemical reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process of CO₂ involves four key steps: (_i_) The diffusion of CO₂ from the bulk solution to the catalyst’s surface, (_ii_) The adsorption of CO₂ on the catalyst surface. (_iii_) The electron-proton transfers (reduction and protonation) to split C-O bonds or form C-H bonds, and finally (_iv_) The detaching of the final products from the electrocatalyst surface to the electrolyte [\[41\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib41), [\[43\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib43).

The rate of CO₂RR is significantly controlled by the kinetics of these four steps. For instance, if the diffusion rate of CO₂ from the bulk of the electrolyte to the electrode surface is slow, it can be a limiting factor for the overall reaction rate. Factors such as the concentration of CO₂ in the solution, the nature of the solvent, and the distance between the CO₂ source and the electrode surface can affect the diffusion rate. Similarly, the adsorption of CO₂ molecules on the catalyst surface is another critical factor. The strength of the interaction between CO₂ and the catalyst surface, often referred to as [adsorption energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/adsorption-energy "Learn more about adsorption energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can vary depending on the catalyst material, and it is extremely critical for both product selectivity and its formation rate. While strong adsorption of CO₂ onto the catalyst surface may enhance the catalytic rate, towards a particular product, it is essential to balance the adsorption strength, to maintain high selectivity towards that product [\[44\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib44). The electron-proton transfers steps involve the actual chemical transformation of CO₂. The kinetics of this step depend on the catalyst's ability to facilitate electron and [proton transfers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/proton-transfer "Learn more about proton transfers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") efficiently [\[45\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib45), [\[46\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib46). Catalyst materials with good electron conductivity and suitable active sites for the reaction can accelerate this step, leading to a higher reaction rate. Finally, once the reaction is complete the products must detach from the surface to enter the electrolyte. The kinetics of detachment can be affected by factors such as the strength of product-catalyst interactions. Faster detachment kinetics can lead to a higher reaction rate. The final rate of CO₂RR is limited by the slowest step among these four processes. [Catalyst design](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/catalyst-design "Learn more about Catalyst design from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and optimization often focus on improving the kinetics of these steps to enhance the overall reaction rate and make CO₂RR more efficient and economically viable. The reduction potentials of various competitive reactions are closely aligned, making it a challenge to create [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysts "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that can fulfill multiple criteria simultaneously, including high selectivity, [current density](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/current-density "Learn more about current density from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), low overpotential, and stability. Moreover, these factors are significantly impacted by electrolyte properties, electrode architecture, and operational conditions like temperature and pressure.

In [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is a distinct form of catalysis, catalysts are employed to facilitate and accelerate chemical reactions that occur at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces in [electrochemical cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrochemical-cell "Learn more about electrochemical cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [\[47\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib47). In these reactions, electrical energy is converted to chemical energy, where anodic and cathodic reactions take place in separate compartments [\[36\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib36). In the context of CO₂ electroreduction, the cathode serves as the site for the main reaction, but the choice of anodic reaction is also vital for economic viability. Consequently, pairing two reactions, each capable of yielding valuable products, holds significant promise for sustainable chemical production and enhancing the overall efficiency of the electrochemical process [\[48\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212982024001549#bib48). ## Abstract

As an important part of carbon neutralization, carbon dioxide electroreduction reaction (CO2RR) can convert CO2 into high value-added chemicals and fuels to realize the recycling of carbon resources and solve the problem of environmental pollution. Therefore, exploring the element species and surface structure of the catalyst plays a central role in improving the performance of the catalyst, enhancing the CO2 conversion efficiency and forming C1 and C2+ products. Here, we summarize the recent progress in the selective regulation of CO2RR reaction products by different elements. In particular, we emphasize the structure-property relationship of CO2RR by the microenvironment of metal center and substrate, heteroatom doping, hydrogen bond network of metal-free polymer, and construction of heterogeneous catalytic system. At the same time, the recent advances for the identification of CO2RR active sites and mechanistic studies on the process of reducing CO2 conversion to different products are reviewed, as well as a comprehensive review to the final products. Finally, we outline the inevitable challenges faced by CO2RR and present our own recommendations aimed at contributing to CO2 resource utilization.

# Catalysts for electrochemical CO2 conversion: material sustainability perspective

[*npj Materials Sustainability*](/npjmatsustain)
**volume 3**, Article number: 22 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The electrochemical reduction of CO2 (eCO2R) presents a promising pathway for addressing climate change by converting CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels. A crucial aspect of this technology is the choice of catalyst materials, which directly influences the selectivity, stability and sustainability of the process. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products, including formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment. We compare more than 68 case studies in eCO2R using various metal-based catalysts. Our results show that Bi-based catalysts for formate production have the highest supply risk and environmental burdens, while Sn-based catalysts show overall better durability and much lower sustainability concerns. Copper-based catalysts’ supply risk for ethylene conversion is lower and more concentrated, whereas the supply risk for ethanol conversion is more dispersed. Our findings further confirm that improving catalyst performance—especially the stability—can substantially mitigate both supply risks and environmental impacts. This highlights the urgent need for standardized methodologies to assess catalyst stability and novel strategies to further improve catalyst stability using both material and system approaches. We call for stronger cross-sector collaboration to further integrate criticality and sustainability assessment frameworks with more granular datasets and dynamic spatial and temporal representation, for continuous eco-design improvement of eCO2R catalysts.

## Introduction

The manufacturing industry is heavily dependent on fossil fuels, both as the primary energy source for high-pressure, high-temperature thermochemical reactions and as key reactants. The CO2 emissions arise not only from fuel combustion but also as by-products of chemical reactions[1](#ref-CR1 "Bui, J. C. et al. Continuum Modeling of Porous Electrodes for Electrochemical Synthesis. Chem. Rev. 122, 11022–11084 (2022)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Andrew, R. M. In 2022, industry sectors contributed up to nearly 38% of global fuel combustion emissions alone, which was approximately 12.9 gigaton of CO2[4](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR4 "Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Energy Data Explorer. 
                  https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-tools/greenhouse-gas-emissions-from-energy-data-explorer
                  
                 (2024)."). These factors make reducing CO2 emissions in the manufacturing industry particularly challenging.

The electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (eCO2R) offers a promising route to produce low-carbon-emission chemicals using CO2, water, and renewable electricity (Fig. [1a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). When CO2 is captured directly from the air, eCO2R has the potential to close the carbon loop for fuels and chemicals that release CO2 during their end use. In recent years, several attempts have been made to scale the CO2 or CO electrolyzer up by two orders of magnitude in size[65](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR65 "Crandall, B. S. et al. Kilowatt-scale tandem CO2 electrolysis for enhanced acetate and ethylene production. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 421–429 (2024)."),[66](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR66 "Nelson, V. E. et al. Scaling CO2 Electrolyzer Cell Area from Bench to Pilot. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 16, 50818–50825 (2024)."). These studies have suggested that catalysts such as copper- or silver-based, with proven performance, are of particular interest for the large-scale deployment of this conversion technology.

Metal-based catalysts are the primary choice for eCO2R due to their high activity and selectivity, but scaling this technology under decarbonization goals introduces material supply risks. Converting 1 ton of CO2 daily would require 170 g of Cu, meaning over 90 tons annually for recycling 1 Gt of CO2[67](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR67 "Belsa, B. et al. Materials challenges on the path to gigatonne CO2 electrolysis. Nat. Rev. Mater. 9, 535–549 (2024)."). As demand for critical metals like Cu, Ag, and Sn grows, the system becomes increasingly vulnerable to price volatility, supply chain disruptions, and geopolitical risks, all of which could undermine its economic viability and hinder large-scale deployment. These supply challenges are compounded by the environmental burdens associated with metal extraction, highlighting the importance of understanding their life-cycle sustainability. However, despite the widespread use of metal-based catalysts in eCO2R, little is known about their supply risks or the environmental burdens linked to the preference for metal selection. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products like formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment.

## Material Requirements for Catalysts in Electrochemical CO2 Conversion

### Direct conversion

The direct conversion of CO2 takes place in a single reactor and converts CO2 into desirable products (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)), such as ethanol, ethylene, and formate[68](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR68 "Gao, G., Obasanjo, C. A., Crane, J. & Dinh, C.-T. Comparative analysis of electrolyzers for electrochemical carbon dioxide conversion. Catal. Today 423 (2023)."). Numerous reactor configurations exist, with the most promising for scale-up being the zero-gap membrane electrode assembly[69](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR69 "Ma, W. et al. Electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 and CO to multi-carbon compounds over Cu-based catalysts. Chem. Soc. Rev. 50, 12897–12914 (2021).") (Fig. [1b](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). Many different types of metals have been employed as catalysts for this reaction. The most promising metal for valuable C2+ products is copper, possibly due to its favourable adsorption energies for both \*CO and \*H[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Metals that benefit the production of other products, such as formate, have also been determined[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Polymer binders are often incorporated into the catalyst layer, which is deposited onto a support, to bind the catalyst to the support and to tune the activity and the selectivity of the reaction[71](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR71 "Pham, T. H. M. et al. Enhanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products by Adjusting the Local Reaction Environment with Polymer Binders. Adv. Energy Mater. 12 (2022).").

When employing an anion exchange membrane, these reactors provide a basic cathodic environment which favours eCO2R[72](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR72 "Salvatore, D. A. et al. Designing anion exchange membranes for CO2 electrolysers. Nat. Energy 6, 339–348 (2021)."). However, this high pH environment also leads to the formation of (bi)carbonates that can cross through the membrane and cause the loss of available reactant and increase of downstream separation costs[73](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR73 "Lees, E. W. et al. Exploring CO2 reduction and crossover in membrane electrode assemblies. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 340–353 (2024)."). Acidic systems may offer a solution to this issue, but they suffer from their own problems, such as an acidic catholyte environment that favours hydrogen evolution reaction over eCO2R[74](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR74 "Habibzadeh, F. et al. Ion Exchange Membranes in Electrochemical CO2 Reduction Processes. Electrochem. Energy Rev. 6 (2023).").

To be economically viable, these electrolyzers require operation at an industrial scale for tens of thousands of hours[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."). However, reported stability performance currently lags and will need to be improved to ensure industrial feasibility. As examples of high product selectivity in the MEA cell configuration, studies have shown Faradaic Efficiencies (FEs) of 52% for ethanol[26](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR26 "Wang, X. et al. Efficient electrically powered CO2-to-ethanol via suppression of deoxygenation. Nat. Energy 5, 478–486 (2020)."), 92.8% for ethylene[22](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR22 "Merino-Garcia, I., Albo, J. & Irabien, A. Stable, active CO2 reduction to formate via redox-modulated stabilization of active sites. Nat. Commun. 12, 5223 (2021)."), and 87% for formic acid[31](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR31 "Fan, L. et al. Electrochemical CO2 reduction to high-concentration pure formic acid solutions in an all-solid-state reactor. Nat. Commun. 11, 3633 (2020)."). Some higher FEs have been reported but in configurations less suited to scale-up, such as 91% for ethanol[29](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR29 "Xu, H. et al. Highly selective electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to ethanol by metallic clusters dynamically formed from atomically dispersed copper. Nat. Nat. Commun. 12, 5223 (2021)."). A summary of different studies across multiple different electrolyzer configurations is shown in Table [S1](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#MOESM1).

### Two-step conversion

The reduction of CO2 generates multiple products, which are carbon monoxide, ethylene, ethanol, formate, acetate, methane and propanol. Many of these products share the same reaction pathway[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."), among these, carbon monoxide is special because it is the common intermediate for the formation of most C2+ products during electrolysis[75](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR75 "Yoshio Hori, Ryutaro Takahashi, Yuzuru Yoshinami, a. & Murata, A. Electrochemical Reduction of CO at a Copper Electrode. J. Phys. Chem. B (1997)."),[76](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR76 "Hori, Y., Murata, A., Takahashi, R. & Suzuki, S. Electroreduction of carbon monoxide to methane and ethylene at a copper electrode in aqueous solutions at ambient temperature and pressure. J. Am. Chem. Soc. (2002).") (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)).

In an eCO2R process using an anion exchange membrane, an alkaline environment on the cathode side is ideal. However, a reaction between CO2 and the hydroxide ions formed during the process produces bicarbonate, resulting in reactant loss. This issue can be avoided with electrochemical CO reduction (eCOR), as CO does not react with hydroxide ions. This allows highly alkaline conditions to be maintained without reactant depletion, offering a key advantage for this method.

Despite this, a technical challenge with eCOR is the low solubility of CO in aqueous electrolyte solutions. To overcome this, the catalyst is designed to enable fast CO transport to the catalyst layer, allowing for current densities higher than 200 mA·cm−2 to be achieved[5](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR5 "O’Brien, C. P. et al. CO2 Electrolyzers. Chem. Rev. 124, 3648–3693 (2024)."). Most eCOR studies at high current densities use gas diffusion electrodes with Nafion and other perfluorosulfonic acid ionomers. These materials have hydrophobic backbones that extend the three-phase (gas, liquid, and solid) boundary into the catalyst layer, enhancing the overall transport of CO to the active sites and maintaining high current density throughout the reaction[59](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR59 "Li, J. et al. ### Material requirements for catalyst

Overall, the choice of catalyst material and its loading depends on the targeted product, with copper (Cu) being predominant for hydrocarbons and alcohols, tin (Sn) and bismuth (Bi) for formate, and silver (Ag) for carbon monoxide production. For the direct production of ethylene and methane from CO2, copper (Cu)-based catalysts are most commonly applied, with loadings typically ranging from 0.25 to 1.25 mg·cm−2 [12](#ref-CR12 "K, X. et al. enriched_points.expanded_content: Catalysts must maximize CO₂ conversion rates while minimizing energy input, achieving high Faradaic efficiency to ensure effective resource utilization and economic viability.
Catalysts should preferentially produce desired products (e.g., ethanol, ethylene) over undesired byproducts, enhancing overall process viability and market value of outputs.
Catalysts require long-term operational stability to minimize degradation, ensuring sustained performance and reduced maintenance costs for industrial applications.
Catalyst production and usage must consider lifecycle assessment, aiming to minimize adverse ecological effects and promote sustainable practices throughout the CO₂ reduction process.
Selecting economically viable materials and processes is crucial, balancing catalyst performance with low material costs to improve profitability in large-scale CO₂ reduction operations.
